description: Optimizing the workspace dashboard query that lists recent documents (no body needed). Review TOAST, storage parameters, index design, and column ordering.
review_checklist: Distinguish STORAGE EXTERNAL vs EXTENDED vs MAIN for large columns
Identify covering index opportunities for fetch-light queries
Consider column ordering for null bitmap and pad alignment

ground_truth.description: Index lacks INCLUDE for the dashboard query. The query fetches `id, title, last_edited_at` — `id` is in the PK index, `last_edited_at` is the sort key, but `title` is not in the index. Each row returned does a heap fetch to get title. With body and yjs_state in the heap row, the heap page is large and rarely cache-resident. Use `CREATE INDEX ... INCLUDE (title)` for an index-only scan.

STORAGE EXTERNAL on `body` forces TOAST without compression. body is collaborative-edit text — highly compressible (>5:1 typical). EXTENDED (default for TEXT) gives compression + out-of-line storage. EXTERNAL only suppresses compression, increasing TOAST table size 5x and slowing reads. Wrong choice. Reverse to EXTENDED or leave at default.

STORAGE EXTERNAL on yjs_state (BYTEA, CRDT binary) is correct — CRDT serialized state is already entropy-coded and incompressible. So EXTERNAL is right here, but only by coincidence. Document the reasoning explicitly in a comment.

ALTER COLUMN ... SET STORAGE on a populated table acquires AccessExclusiveLock and rewrites EVERY existing row's TOAST entries to match the new strategy. For a 12M-document table where bodies average 800KB, this is several TB of data to rewrite. Multi-day outage. PG does not perform this lazily — the rewrite is eager.

Adding preview_text VARCHAR(280) without DEFAULT makes the column nullable initially. Architecturally better: use GENERATED ALWAYS AS (LEFT(body, 280)) STORED — automatic, can never drift. The current approach requires application logic to maintain it.

Column ordering inefficiency: BIGINT, VARCHAR(300), TEXT, BYTEA, INTEGER, INTEGER, BOOLEAN, TIMESTAMPTZ... mixes 8-byte and smaller types. PostgreSQL pads each column to alignment boundaries — the BOOLEAN after BIGINT wastes 7 bytes per row of padding. Reorder to fixed-width-first: BIGINT, INTEGER, INTEGER, TIMESTAMPTZ x2, BOOLEAN, then variable-length. On 12M rows this saves ~80MB and improves cache density.
